summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / anti-fascism, Democracy / press freedom, Games / criticism. The strongest items in this run are Inside 5 Rural Texas Districts That Together Set Students on Path to the Future, These blind students say their college blocked their education. A new rule could help, and A new start after 60: I jacked in my job in tech to become a professional poker player.

mainItems.title: Inside 5 Rural Texas Districts That Together Set Students on Path to the Future
mainItems.summary: Each day, hundreds of rural south Texas high schoolers wake before sunrise to board vans that bump for miles over back roads, crossing ranch land and thickets of brush.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: These blind students say their college blocked their education. A new rule could help
mainItems.summary: Higher education is especially reliant on computers and phones, but accessibility for people with disabilities has often been forgotten. A new federal rule could change that.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: A new start after 60: I jacked in my job in tech to become a professional poker player
mainItems.summary: As a child, Gary Fisher was terrible at card games. How did he end up making his living from one? Gary Fisher has always enjoyed a game of poker, but after he turned 60, his partner suggested he take it seriously.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: We've gone mad for puzzles. This makes sense – it’s reassuring to have answers in these perplexing times | Joseph de Weck
mainItems.summary: Our world feels chaotic, confusing and unfair, but puzzles offer clear rules, solvable problems and reward for effort expended Maybe you’ve noticed it too. Everyone seems to have become fixated on puzzle games.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: The best videogame characters who came back from the dead
mainItems.summary: Greetings, loyal Treehouse tenders and Horace huggers! Today is a bank holiday in the UK, which means we are probably asleep, but we've scraped together a little themed conversation piece article to comfort you in our absence.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: ‘It started with a tipoff’: how a Guardian investigation exposed child sex trafficking on Facebook and Instagram
mainItems.summary: Meta has just lost a multimillion-dollar legal battle over its failure to prevent children being sold on its platforms. Here’s how we uncovered evidence that became part of the case against it It started with a tipoff.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to systems, infrastructure, and technical risk.

mainItems.title: My mother, Audrey Hepburn: the star’s son Sean on her movies, marriages, good works and fascist parents
mainItems.summary: The heroine of Roman Holiday and Breakfast at Tiffany’s knew war and poverty, riches and fame, love and betrayal – yet claimed to have lived a ‘terribly boring’ life.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Sony's battle against shovelware publishers persists as it purges another load of crap games from the PlayStation Store
mainItems.summary: Sony has once again delisted a number of games from its storefronts, including the catalogues of alleged shovelware publishers like GoGame Console Publisher, VRCForge Studios, and Welding Byte. Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Pencil Running for Oregon Governor Hopes to Make Its Mark on Education Issues
mainItems.summary: Oregon’s low youth literacy rates spurred a new candidate to announce a write-in campaign for governor on Monday. Most Oregonians have at least some experience with the candidate, but it’s never held elected office before.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Middle East crisis live: Iran warns of ‘devastating’ retaliation after Trump’s expletive-laden threats over strait of Hormuz
mainItems.summary: US and Iran reportedly receive proposal for a potential 45-day ceasefire; IRGC says Majid Khademi was killed early on Monday Trump warns Iran to reopen strait of Hormuz by Tuesday or face ‘hell’ ‘Unhinged madman’: US politicians react to…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Los Thuthanaka’s Wak’a is a mellower follow-up to last year’s surprise Pitchfork favorite
mainItems.summary: Los Thuthanaka basically came out of nowhere last year to capture Pitchfork's album of the year with their self-titled debut. Because it wasn't available on streaming, it largely flew under the radar.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Slay the Spire 2 beats Crimson Desert and Resident Evil Requiem as March's biggest-selling game on Steam
mainItems.summary: Slay the Spire 2 was allegedly March's biggest-selling game on Steam, shifting more units than other new releases like Crimson Desert and Resident Evil Requiem, bringing an estimated revenue of around $108 million (£81.7 million). Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Useful if you care about how games actually work as designed experiences.

mainItems.title: Amid a high-profile scandal, Germany considers deepfake porn punishments
mainItems.summary: Congress passed the Take It Down Act in 2024, protecting victims of deepfake revenge pornography. Now, Germany is considering punishing the creators of deepfake porn, not just the distributors, for up to 2 years.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Interesting where engineering, science, and tech infrastructure collide.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Deadwood
older.why: If you want language, moral complexity, and character density more than genre comfort, this is a strong older miss possibility.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.
